review: Civilization VI is one of those games where you tell yourself you'll only play for an hour, and suddenly it’s 3 AM. Every civilization feels unique, fun to play, and no two playthroughs ever feel the same. The replayability is honestly insane, especially with the DLC. Definitely one of the most addicting strategy games I’ve played.

[b]📅 [Дата релиза]:[/b] 21 октября 2016
[b]🏢 [Студия]:[/b] Firaxis Games
[b]⚙️ [Движок]:[/b] Firaxis
[b]⌛️  [Время прохождения]:[/b] ~50+ часов (бесконечная реиграбельность)
[b]⚙️ [Тип]:[/b] Пошаговая RTS
[b]🎭 [Жанр]:[/b] Исторический / Стратегия
[b]📖 [Сэттинг]:[/b] Земля (разные эпохи)
[b]🌌 [Франшиза]:[/b] Civilization
[b]👤 [Протагонист]:[/b] Кастомный лидер нации
[b]🦹 [Антагонист]:[/b] Лидеры враждебных наций/Варвары

[b]👍 [Что понравилось]:[/b] Большая глубина механик с разделением городов на районы, хороший саундтрек, который эволюционирует вместе с эпохами, и мощный затягивающий геймплей по принципу «еще один ход и спать».
[b]👎 [Что НЕ понравилось]:[/b] Местами откровенно глупый искусственный интеллект противников, перегруженный и хаотичный дипломатический конгресс, а также излишне мультяшный визуальный стиль по сравнению с пятой частью, который зайдет не всем.

[b]💭 [Личное впечатление]:[/b] Потрясающая в своей залипательности игра, которую каждый раз интересно именно стартовать по новой хех. Я устану вспоминать даже как много раз я стартовал новые партии и каждый раз с восторгом углублялся то в культурную победу, то в военную, то в научную, то чисто себе строил в кайф городки на популяцию и шедевры. В этом плане игра всегда была крутой песочницей, где ты можешь строить свою империю и развивать в любом направлении. А вот доигрывать прям до талого я редко оставался, каюсь) С каждой эпохой конеш партия приедается и мне лично хотелось по новой начать. После V конечно на релизе игра смотрелась дико сырой и мне прям были неприятные эти деления городов на райончики да, но к этому быстро привыкаешь и темка интересная в любом случаи. Норм так в нее погамал, и с друзьями и в соло и до сих пор считаю её одной из своих любимых игр.

review_english: hr hr Title b Sid Meier's Civilization VI b Release date b October 21, 2016 b Studio b Firaxis Games b Engine b Firaxis b Time b 50 hours (infinite replayability) b Type b Turn-based RTS b Genre b Historical Strategy b Setting b Earth (different eras) b Franchise b Civilization b Protagonist b Custom leader of a nation b Antagonist b Leaders of hostile nations Barbarians b What we liked b Great depth of mechanics with the division of cities into districts, a good soundtrack that evolves with the eras, and powerful addictive gameplay based on the principle of one more turn and sleep. b What we didn’t like b The frankly stupid artificial intelligence of the enemies in places, the overloaded and chaotic diplomatic congress, as well as the overly cartoonish visual style compared to the fifth part, which will not appeal to everyone. b Personal impression b A stunning game in its stickiness, which is interesting to start with a new one every time heh. I’ll even get tired of remembering how many times I started new games and each time with delight I delved into a cultural victory, then a military one, then a scientific one, or I built towns for my own pleasure for the population and masterpieces. In this regard, the game has always been a cool sandbox, where you can build your empire and develop it in any direction. But I rarely stayed until the end of the game, I confess) With each era, the game becomes boring, and I personally wanted to start anew. After V, of course, at release the game looked wildly crude and I really didn’t like these divisions of cities into districts, yes, but you quickly get used to it and the topic is interesting in any case. Norm played it so much, both with friends and solo, and I still consider it one of my favorite games.

review:
Personal Rating: Very Positive (90%)

I spent my entire morning and afternoon by playing this today.

I used to really hate the hexagonal tile design, as it looked really dumb to me. But after giving it one more shot today for... whatever reason, I actually really like it.

By gameplay, it's somewhere in between the old AoH2 and HoI4, and that's pretty much ideal. Pretty challenging, but not overcomplicated.

I can see myself playing further, as it's really enjoyable and time passes on really quickly when playing. But I don't know if I'll go for Civ 7 or any of the DLC's.

review:
After finally passing 1,000 hours playtime for this, I figured I should write a review.

Civilization games function best when they allow sandbox gameplay to tell a story on its own. From the very beginnings of each game, you feel invested in your civilization and can understand how beliefs / religion can form from the natural habitat around you and the benefits it offers. Starting near floodplains can lead to you worshipping the rivers and marshes and the food they bring, starting in harsher climates such as the desert or tundra can strongly alter the culture of a country. You can develop long lasting rivalries with opposing civilizations and bare grudges for hundreds of years over past slights.

Gameplay wise each of the different win conditions is varied enough to allow you to choose a different path through each playthrough. Domination can be a slow grind towards the end as you finish off the last opponents, religion seems like a slightly more limited version of domination but does have some variety. Science victory is the least reliant on interaction with the AI and so feels like a good fallback option if other objectives haven't gone your way early. Culture wins are the most varied simply because of all the different ways of getting culture / tourism and the different mechanics that feed into it.

The last Civilization game I played a lot of was 2, so I initially approached the combat in 6 with the same brute force unit spam tactics. Fortunately things have moved on since then, you have to use the terrain and the rock/paper/scissors mechanics of the unit types to your advantage. Only forts seem to be largely useless.

One of the best things about this game is how history is brought to life within it. It was a joy to pick up on all the familiar names, from Mary Shelley's / Jane Austen's / Leo Tolstoy's books to the paintings of Andrei Rublev (as I was aware through the Andrei Tarkovsky film of his life). Even figures I became aware of such as Kristina of Sweden (depicted by Liv Ulman in The Abdication) and Eleanor of Aquitane (depicted by Katharine Hepburn in The Lion In Winter). The more you learn about the different cultures, the more you feel tied to them in the game.

If I had to voice one frustration with the game, it's that the AI doesn't play the game well enough so the difficulty levels are compensated by giving them extreme bonuses in terms of unit numbers at the start of the game. This creates a slightly skewed gameplay curve where the biggest challenge is surviving and playing catchup at the start and yet by about halfway through your victory can feel somewhat inevitable because the AI is incapable of building districts correctly and focusing on win conditions.

Overall I can't recommend the game and all the expansions enough. I played for a while without the addons and felt satisfied, but once I got them it was like I had an almost entirely new game to learn and enjoy.

review:
아직얼마 안한시점이긴한데(100시간도 안되었으니)뭔가 엄청 복잡하다....고 생각했는데 따지고보면 문명은 원래 이랬지 5가 너무 간단깔끔캐주얼했어......

하지먼 인터페이스 진짜 뭐같다... 특히 맵상에서 시설 뭐 지었는지도 알아보기 힘든거랑 위인 발동시키려고 특수지구 찾아다니는거....(특히 예술계 걸작슬롯 찾아댕기기....)
그외에 글자 빽빽해서 내용 다 외기전엔 이거 계속 읽고 파악해야헤서 피곤할거같다(...)

그리고 초기 시절 야만인과 적문명 알박기 짜증나... 뭐냐 이 웨이브 디펜스 하는 느낌은........
그렇다고 우방하면... 땅이 좁아진다... 이번작은 5랑달라 특수지구랑 이런저런거 실제 타일에 지어야 해서 원시티플레이같은건 못한다고....

그리고 외교는 이거 뭐가 트리거인가 싶다... 상대는 우리 군대 근처 지나간다고 찡얼대고 종교퍼뜨리지 말라고 찡얼대고 근처에  도시박지 말라고 찡얼대고 살살 달래다 삐긋실수하면 약속 어겼다고 난리치는데(강하면 전쟁선포) 누이는 왜 말을 못하나.....

게다가 덤으로 전쟁해서 도시점령하면 충성도 조정도짜증난다... 총독박을틈도없이 반란 일으키는 위치의 도시는 뭐지...그냥 파괴하라는건가......

...................그런데 7이 워낙에 엉망이라 이정도면 나름이라고 해야한다는 소리를 듣고있다는데 7은 쳐다보지 말아야하나;;;;

일단 시간잡아먹을 정도 재미는 있으니 추천은 해둔다. 솔직히 문명은 4가 완성판 이라고 생각한다...고 하면 돌맞겠지....(최소한 사각이아니라 헥사맵이었다면 더 당당했을지도)

review_english: It's not that long ago (less than 100 hours), but I thought something was very complicated... but when you think about it, Civilization was originally like this, Civilization 5 was too simple, neat, and casual... but the interface is really like... especially, it's hard to see what facilities were built on the map, and searching for special districts to activate great people... (especially searching for art masterpiece slots...) Other than that, the text is so dense that it's hard to memorize everything. I guess I'm tired from having to keep reading and understanding this (...) And in the early days, it's annoying to be stuck with barbarians and enemy civilizations... What is this feeling of playing wave defense... But if you're an ally... the land gets smaller... In this game, you have to build 5-Rang Dalla special districts and other things on actual tiles, so you can't do one-city play... And I wonder what kind of trigger this is for diplomacy... The opponent whines about passing near our army and whines about not spreading religion. He whines and gently comforts her, telling her not to attack the city nearby, but if she pouts, she'll yell at him for breaking his promise (if he's too strong, he'll declare war), but why can't she say anything... Plus, if you go to war and take over the city, adjusting loyalty is also annoying... What's wrong with a city that's in a position to rebel without even giving the governor a chance... Are you saying just destroy it... But I'm hearing that 7 is in such a mess that it should be taken care of. Should I stop looking at 7? First of all, it's fun enough to take up time, so I recommend it. To be honest, I think Civilization 4 is the complete version... If I say that, I'll be stoned... (at least, it might have been more confident if it was a hexamap instead of a square one)

review:
Review pocket
Vou falar apenas do que vi. Leve esta review como alguém que não está tão inserido no nicho de jogos de estratégia.

Barreira Inicial
De início, as incontáveis abstrações numéricas e períodos intermináveis de produção de cada projeto realmente faz você entrar em parafuso. Mas quando começa a entender, esse desenvolvimento próprio é ótimo. O problema é que o tutorial te joga tudo em textos desinteressantes e não mostra ou treina a filosofia da própria estratégia.

"Só mais um turno..."
Quando você estabelece objetivos internos, o ciclo de gameplay te convence que esses objetivos são importantes para o jogo, traz a vontade e paciência para atingi-lo e te recompensa muito bem ao fim de tudo caso tenha aplicado o senso estratégico de maneira efetiva. Ou seja, quando você compra a proposta, esse jogo que te traz ""imersão"" muito forte. O tempo voa numa partida.

Sound Tracks / Effects
Ele é muito bom! Todos os sfx são reconhecíveis e as músicas são incrívelmente ótimas. Cada nação tem seus motiffs (baseado em músicas reais aparentemente) que é sempre derivada nos 4 estágios de eras. Ao decorrer dessas 4 eras, a composição vai gradualmente aderindo a mais instrumentos e ficando cada vez mais épica. Isso transmite uma sensação de desenvolvimento ao mesmo tempo que transborda a personalidade de cada império!

review_english: Review pocket I'll just talk about what I saw. Take this review as someone who is not so immersed in the strategy games niche. Initial Hurdle At first, the countless numerical abstractions and endless production periods for each project really send you into a tailspin. But when you start to understand, this self-development is great. The problem is that the tutorial throws everything at you in uninteresting texts and doesn't show or train the philosophy of the strategy itself. "Just one more turn..." When you establish internal objectives, the gameplay cycle convinces you that these objectives are important for the game, brings the will and patience to achieve them and rewards you very well at the end of it all if you have applied strategic sense effectively. In other words, when you buy the proposal, this game brings you a very strong ""immersion"". Time flies in a match. Sound Tracks Effects It's very good! All the sfx are recognizable and the songs are incredibly great. Each nation has its motiffs (based on real music apparently) which is always derived from the 4 stages of eras. Over the course of these 4 eras, the composition gradually incorporates more instruments and becomes increasingly epic. This conveys a sense of development while brimming with the personality of each empire!

É um bom jogo, dá pra se divertir por um bom tempo. Acho que uma das coisas que mais incomoda no final das contas é a IA do jogo. Os NPC's ficam bravos porque seu exército é pequeno (????), porque você não tem frotas navais o suficiente e até porque tem poucos territórios. As negociações com os NPC's também são bem meia boca e o conselho das nações também poderia ser muito melhor. Tem coisa que está no jogo só pra dizer que existe, mas que na prática a execução é péssima.

De todo modo, é um bom jogo, tanto para jogar sozinho quanto para jogar com os amigos.

review:
Modded Civ 6 is a perfectly fine game, but I personally feel like Civ 5 with the Vox Populi mod is a better game overall.

Civ 6 (unmodded) is beautiful and smooth game (in terms of things loading and happening) with a fantastic government system that's completely bogged down by notification hell. There's just way to many damn annoying things to do all the time in the game, even if you're winning by a landslide. Religion units everywhere, that god awful rock band noise on loop, selecting envoys, random boosts, some civ leader needing to finish their one liner, UI being pretty terrible without mods, etc.

Pacing feels way off given how neat and restricting districts are. I like them in concept, but you're specializing in districts at first simply cause the time to invest in them is extremely long vs. the amount of troops you may need to fend off barbarians (or other civs). If things settle, then it's off to the moon in terms of how fast things develop.

I can say that the only reason I'd play Civ 6 is a more stable platform for multiplayer.

Civ 6 (unmodded) is beautiful and smooth game (in terms of things loading and happening) with a fantastic government system that's completely bogged down by notification hell. There's just way to many damn annoying things to do all the time in the game, even if you're winning by a landslide. Religion units everywhere, that god awful rock band noise on loop, selecting envoys, random boosts, some civ leader needing to finish their one liner, UI being pretty terrible without mods, etc. Pacing feels way off given how neat and restricting districts are. I like them in concept, but you're specializing in districts at first simply cause the time to invest in them is extremely long vs. the amount of troops you may need to fend off barbarians (or other civs). If things settle, then it's off to the moon in terms of how fast things develop. I can say that the only reason I'd play Civ 6 is a more stable platform for multiplayer.

review:
Amo y odio este juego tanto como Gollum ama y odia el anillo.

Siempre me gustaron los juegos de estrategia por turnos, y este fue el primer Civilization al que jugué.
Lo adquirí gratuitamente a través de Epic Games.
Al inicio todo era nuevo: mecánicas nuevas, música increíble, mapas procedurales, estética muy amigable, aprenderme las ramas de ciencia y cultura, las adyacencias de los distritos...

Tras practicar y entender las bases empecé a ponerlas a prueba. Me costó, pero un día gané con España una partida con IAs en Deidad (fueron +5h seguidas sin parar). Luego perdí varias veces hasta asentar los conocimientos necesarios para ganar contra la IA.

Durante el proceso compartía mi progreso con un amigo, jugando con él y finalmente contra él...
Aquí empezó un nuevo mundo. Si ya era divertido intentar ganar en Deidad, ahora iba a serlo más compitiendo con mi amigo. Cada vez que uno perdía el ganador se relajaba y el perdedor tenía que actualizarse y mejorar.

Fue divertido mucho tiempo, hasta que llegas aprox. a las 1000h y te das cuenta que contra jugadores las civilizaciones no están nada balanceadas... Bastó usar a Gorgo 2 o 3 partidas para que mi amigo y yo nos diésemos cuenta. Acabamos baneandola nosotros mismos por lo rota que estaba.

Entonces descubrimos BBG y la comunidad CPL. El gran problema de Civ6 es el online: no hay rankeds, la gente se va de las partidas por no llegar a la primera época dorada, no hay banneos por abandonar pronto, desbalance de civis, mil tipos de bugs de desincronización infames... y un largo etc. BBG solucionó (más o menos) el balanceo, por lo menos le da soporte. La comunidad CPL no arregla los problemas que trae el juego, pero tiene guías de cómo operar en cada tipo de incidencia.

Si tuviera que recomendar Civ6 hoy... Por menos de 15€ es tolerable si se trata de la versión Antology. Digo tolerable porque la forma en que yo empecé a jugar es muy particular (con un amigo, ambos flipando con un nuevo género y compitiendo entre nosotros). Es un juego antiguo porque no hay soporte y ya existe Civ7, pero la comunidad sigue viva mientras Civ7 no se asiente definitivamente. Además tiene una estética muy distinta a otras entregas y no es mala forma de empezar a aprender mecánicas comunes de la saga.

A mi me ha dado muchas risas e ilusión en cada partida... Todavía juego mientras Civ7 no baje de precio, por los mods y por la comunidad, pero es innegable: hoy en día se demanda multijugador competitivo de calidad, sobre todo en un juego sin modo historia.

review_english: I love and hate this game as much as Gollum loves and hates the ring. I always liked turn-based strategy games, and this was the first Civilization I played. I acquired it for free through Epic Games. At the beginning everything was new, new mechanics, incredible music, procedural maps, very friendly aesthetics, learning the branches of science and culture, the adjacencies of the districts... After practicing and understanding the bases, I began to put them to the test. It was difficult for me, but one day I won a game with Spain with AIs in Deity (it was 5 hours in a row without stopping). Then I lost several times until I established the knowledge necessary to win against the AI. During the process I shared my progress with a friend, playing with him and finally against him... Here a new world began. If it was already fun trying to win in Deity, now it was going to be even more fun competing with my friend. Every time one lost the winner relaxed and the loser had to update and improve. It was fun for a long time, until you arrived approx. at 1000h and you realize that against players the civilizations are not balanced at all... It was enough to use Gorgo for 2 or 3 games for my friend and I to realize it. We ended up banning it ourselves because of how broken it was. Then we discovered BBG and the CPL community. The big problem with Civ6 is the online, there are no rankeds, people leave the games for not reaching the first golden age, there are no bans for leaving early, civis imbalance, a thousand types of infamous desynchronization bugs... and a long etc. BBG solved (more or less) the balancing, at least it supports it. The CPL community does not fix the problems that the game brings, but it has guides on how to operate in each type of incident. If I had to recommend Civ6 today... For less than 15 it's tolerable if it's the Anthology version. I say tolerable because the way I started playing is very particular (with a friend, both of us freaking out about a new genre and competing against each other). It is an old game because there is no support and Civ7 already exists, but the community is still alive while Civ7 is not definitively established. It also has a very different aesthetic from other installments and is not a bad way to start learning common mechanics of the saga. It has given me a lot of laughter and excitement in each game... I still play as long as Civ7 does not drop in price, because of the mods and because of the community, but it is undeniable today that quality competitive multiplayer is in demand, especially in a game without a story mode.

Don't get me wrong, this game is fun because it's Civ. The gameplay of Civ is inherently fun to me. But while the game has that going for it, everything else makes it worse. 

The more cartoonish artstyle makes it feel less serious to me. The UI and apperance of menus are better and more immersive in Civ V in my opinion. 

I generally feel that there's a feeling of higher quality in Civ V compared to this game.

review:
Отыграл 100 часов меньше чем за месяц и я изучил все изьяны игры.
За это время я получил большое удовольствие от игры, пробовал много различных тактик начиная с военного доминирования до захвата городов соседа с помощью лояльности (тут был пик кайфа). Начнем с того что в игру на самом деле легко вкатиться и как стратегия она имеет много разных механик но с помощью интернета все это быстро изучается лишь-бы время да желание. Но и тут открываются много минусов у игры, самый главный это заебность в лейт гейме, если вы бывалый геймер то вам не тяжело представить 10+ городов которыми нужно управлять и строить в них районы с чудесами и т.д., порой просто устаешь и хочешь дропнуть сейв и начать новый. Второй минус это что главные механики игры продаются как в ДЛС, покупая игру за 50 гривен я ожидал подвоха но не такого что чтобы мне открыть все главные механики игры мне нужно заплатить в 12 раз больше чем стоит сама игра (В это входит 2 ДЛС с механиками и куча всяких мини дополнений с новыми нациями, так еще разрабы молодцы что все имба нации они впихнули именно в платный контент). Третий минус это просто не интересная сложность, вот когда я подумал что в игре что-то понимаю решил выставить сложность в новой партии на Божество и просто аххуел, изначально я не знал всего прикола и когда смотрел как соседи во всем лучше меня в 2-3 раза был просто удивлен, но когда я нашел таблицу с показателями что получают вражеские цивы на Божестве то просто был в шоке, именно то что цивкам дают в полтора раза больше всего и у них изначальная цель скрипта строить города как можно ближе к тебе (чтобы зажать тебя на мапе и не дать строить города), ну а если ты вдруг посмеешь построить свой город чуть ближе к ним к тебе стягиваются 8 отрядов войнов и твой город благополучно переходит под вражеский контроль, плюс берем то что варвары в этой игры тоже очень умные и очень любят ставить свои лагеря в упор к цивилизации игрока и не дай бог ты дашь их разведчику увидеть твой город на тебя так-же попрет 5 отрядов противника, +игра любит давать даунские спавны но всегда она спавнит тебя с самого края карты в то время как врагов ближе всего к центру чтобы им было легче расселяться, в общем и целом сложность Божество это не какая не сложность а просто огромный начальный бафф для всех твоих вражеских цив, но даже так если ты переживешь все эти напасти ты с вероятность 90% победишь свою партию т.к. ИИ других цивилизаций остался на том же уровне низких сложностей. Хоть я и расписал весь отзыв о негативных механиках этой игры я не скажу что она плохая а скорее хорошая игра т.к. мои положительные эмоции пересилили негативные. Благо много проблем решаются модами т.к. комьюнити в этой игре и до сих пор развивается

review_english: I played 100 hours in less than a month and I learned all the flaws in the game. During this time, I had a lot of fun with the game, tried many different tactics, from military domination to capturing a neighbor’s cities with the help of loyalty (this was the peak of the thrill). Let's start with the fact that the game is actually easy to get into and as a strategy it has many different mechanics, but with the help of the Internet, all this can be quickly learned if you have time and desire. But even here there are many disadvantages of the game, the most important one is that it sucks in the late game. If you are an experienced gamer, then it’s not hard for you to imagine 10 cities that you need to manage and build districts with miracles in them, etc., sometimes you just get tired and want to drop the save and start a new one. The second minus is that the main mechanics of the game are sold as DLS, buying the game for 50 hryvnia, I expected a catch, but not such that in order to open all the main mechanics of the game I need to pay 12 times more than the cost of the game itself (This includes 2 DLS with mechanics and a bunch of all sorts of mini add-ons with new nations, and the developers are also great that they crammed all the imba nations into paid content). The third minus is simply not an interesting difficulty, so when I thought that I understand something in the game, I decided to set the difficulty in the new game to the Deity and just gasped, initially I didn’t know all the jokes and when I looked at how my neighbors were 2-3 times better than me in everything, I was simply surprised, but when I found a table with the indicators of what enemy civs get on the Deity, I was simply shocked, exactly what civs are given one and a half times more than anything and they have the original goal of the script build cities as close to you as possible (in order to squeeze you on the map and not allow you to build cities), but if you suddenly dare to build your city a little closer to them, 8 squads of warriors are drawn up to you and your city safely comes under enemy control, plus we take the fact that the barbarians in this game are also very smart and really like to set up their camps point-blank to the player’s civilization and God forbid you let their scout see your city, 5 squads will also trample on you enemy, the game likes to give down spawns, but it always spawns you from the very edge of the map, while the enemies are closest to the center so that it is easier for them to settle, in general, the Divinity difficulty is not some kind of difficulty, but just a huge initial buff for all your enemy civs, but even so, if you survive all these misfortunes, you have a 90 probability of defeating your party because The AI ​​of other civilizations remained at the same level of low difficulties. Although I wrote the entire review about the negative mechanics of this game, I won’t say that it is bad, but rather a good game because... my positive emotions outweighed the negative ones. Fortunately, many problems are solved by mods because... community in this game and is still developing

review:
Civilization VI Review

Civilization VI is one of the best strategy games ever made, and easily one of the strongest entries in the Civ series.

It is the sort of game where you sit down for “one quick turn” and then suddenly it is 3am, your tea is cold, your family has forgotten what you look like, and Gandhi is somehow threatening you with nuclear weapons again.

Civ VI gets so much right. The cities feel alive, the districts add real planning, the leaders have personality, and every game tells its own weird little story.

One minute you are peacefully building libraries and trade routes.

The next, you are sending tanks into France because they settled one tile too close to your bananas 2,000 years ago.

That is proper Civilization.

With Civ VII, there are some genuinely good ideas, but for me it has strayed too far from what made the series so addictive in the first place. Anyone looking at where to copy, where not to copy, and what made Civilization work so well should spend serious time looking at Civ VI.

This is the one that understood the magic.

Deep strategy.
Massive replay value.
Endless “just one more turn” moments.
Thousands of hours lost to building wonders, managing cities, and pretending your wars are defensive.

Civ VI is not perfect, but it is brilliant.

10/10.

Would lose an entire weekend to building the Pyramids again.
review_clean: Civilization VI Review Civilization VI is one of the best strategy games ever made, and easily one of the strongest entries in the Civ series. It is the sort of game where you sit down for one quick turn and then suddenly it is 3am, your tea is cold, your family has forgotten what you look like, and Gandhi is somehow threatening you with nuclear weapons again. Civ VI gets so much right. The cities feel alive, the districts add real planning, the leaders have personality, and every game tells its own weird little story. One minute you are peacefully building libraries and trade routes. The next, you are sending tanks into France because they settled one tile too close to your bananas 2,000 years ago. That is proper Civilization. With Civ VII, there are some genuinely good ideas, but for me it has strayed too far from what made the series so addictive in the first place. Anyone looking at where to copy, where not to copy, and what made Civilization work so well should spend serious time looking at Civ VI. This is the one that understood the magic. Deep strategy. Massive replay value. Endless just one more turn moments. Thousands of hours lost to building wonders, managing cities, and pretending your wars are defensive. Civ VI is not perfect, but it is brilliant. 10 10. Would lose an entire weekend to building the Pyramids again.

review:
There is no way one review could describe the scope of this game, but I will try anyways. Widely recognized as one of the greatest 4X games of all time, Civ 6 has it all. From the ancient past to the near future, you will spread religion, wage war, and compete in culture and science to be the greatest Civilization. I don't play a lot of video games (under an hour each day), but I have still played 200 hours of this. There is simply no other game that could possibly rival it. It is engaging throughout, easy to learn, and has plenty of challenges to keep you interested beyond your 100th hour.

As with any game, there are a few cons. The AI aren't the most challenging, and after you reach a certain skill level you will find that they are easy to beat at any difficulty. The game, for being 10 years old, is still at full price. While that's certainly justified, it could be a deterrent, except for the fact that it frequently goes on sale for under $10!

WARNING: THIS PRODUCT CONTAINS CIV6, A HIGHLY ADDICTIVE SUBSTANCE.

review:
Si estás buscando un juego para pasar un ratito y luego seguir con tu vida productiva... huye. En serio. El famoso 'sólo un turno más' en este juego es real. Te dices a ti mismo: 'Bueno, juego 20 turnos más y guardo', pero cuando te quieres dar cuenta, el juego te atrapó por completo y se te pasaron dos o tres horas como si nada.

Mi estilo va más por construir y avanzar: me encantan las victorias científicas, culturales y diplomáticas. Para mí, la verdadera magia del juego está en planificar tus ciudades y construir maravillas (hacer que Pachacútec construya la Torre Eiffel en medio de las montañas no tiene precio). Las victorias por dominación o religión me dan flojera, prefiero mil veces ver crecer mi imperio en paz. Con los bárbaros, de hecho, aplico la de 'si no te metes con ellos, ellos no se meten contigo'.

El verdadero dolor de cabeza es la IA bipolar. Un día todo bien y al siguiente se alían tres líderes para destruirte solo porque produces más cultura o ciencia que ellos. Y claro, como juegas pacífico y no tienes un ejército gigante, también se molestan y te atacan.

¿Lo malo? Si juegas en mapas muy grandes, prepárate para que los turnos tarden una eternidad en pasar a medida que avanza la partida.

Aun así, con más de 400 horas encima y siendo fanático de la saga desde hace tiempo, te digo que vale muchísimo la pena. Es el juego perfecto para desconectar y pasar el rato en cualquier momento. Eso sí, te aconsejo buscarlo cuando esté con una buena rebaja y compra las versiones completas con todas las expansiones, porque ahí es donde realmente el juego se pone bueno.

Recomendadísimo, pero bajo tu propio riesgo de perder la noción del tiempo.

review_english: If you're looking for a game to spend a little while and then continue with your productive life... run away. Oh really. The famous 'just one more turn' in this game is real. You tell yourself 'Well, I'll play 20 more turns and save', but before you know it, the game has completely caught you and two or three hours have passed by as if nothing had happened. My style is more about building and advancing. I love scientific, cultural and diplomatic victories. For me, the real magic of the game is in planning your cities and building wonders (having Pachacutec build the Eiffel Tower in the middle of the mountains is priceless). Victories by domination or religion make me lazy, I would much rather see my empire grow in peace. With barbarians, in fact, I apply 'if you don't mess with them, they won't mess with you'. The real headache is bipolar AI. One day everything is fine and the next three leaders join forces to destroy you just because you produce more culture or science than them. And of course, since you play peacefully and don't have a giant army, they also get upset and attack you. Bad? If you play on very large maps, be prepared for turns to take forever to pass as the game progresses. Even so, with more than 400 hours under my belt and being a fan of the saga for a long time, I tell you that it is extremely worth it. It is the perfect game to disconnect and pass the time at any time. Of course, I advise you to look for it when it is at a good discount and buy the complete versions with all the expansions, because that is where the game really gets good. Highly recommended, but at your own risk of losing track of time.

review: I've been playing Civilization since I was eight years old, Civ III was my first game, and I went back to play the others. This is by far the most player friendly, and mods make it so that if you enjoy the older Civ games more, you can have similar mechanics and graphics in this game. It's my favorite right now not just because of the graphics, but I really enjoy the newer mechanics as well. I still go back to play Civ V or Colonization from time to time, but Civ VI is a very solid game and well worth playing.

review:
I bought this game when it came on sale as kind of a homage to my misspent youth playing Civ 2.  Ah, Civ 2.  I loved that game.  Sure, the AI would build immense stacks of units; I remember.  But at it's core, though, it made some sense.  This game makes me want to scream.

I am the kind of gamer who, when I play, I want to WIN.  So I play on easy level.  As you can see, I've played this game a bit.  92 hours as of the time of writing here.  The vast bulk of that was on one single game.  Which, thinking back, still just makes me want to scream.

Starting the game, some other civ is super close to me, and before a dozen turns are over, it gives up and asks to join my civilization?  How is that supposed to work? And then, for the rest of the game, I'm penalized for that?  The hell? Thanks for the "boost", game, which turned out to be a curse.

I chose an archipelago map, because I loved sending those poor triremes out to be the first to make contact with other continents and cultures.  What does the game do?  Plops me down in the middle of a huge continent, which literally stretches from pole to pole, and not only that, but puts me on a lake, which it calls a "bay" but is not, with other independent cities and civs hogging all the land near the actual ocean, and of course none of those idiots build cities on the two isthmuses that would allow ships to get in and out of the lake.

There was another nearby civilization that just could not get it's crap together.  Another player came and conquered its capital, but could not keep it due to "loyalty", and it rebelled.  And just sat there as an independent city for ~1000 years.  An independent city, mind you, that I could not actually send envoys to or do anything useful with.  Just sitting there.  Since it's on the other side of the continent with access to the ocean on the other side of the world from what I have, I decide it's time to take them out.  My control lasts, like, 3 moves, when loyalty takes it back to its original owner.

This had to be a bug.

I really do not understand how the whole suzerain system works.  So, these other cities just sit there, doing nothing, until I pay them to take their armies?  Huh?  That's the only thing they're good for, other than blocking my access to the ocean?  I dutifully waited until other civs made the most convenient ones suzerains, then wars began, and I took them over -- only to immediately get dinged by some "World Congress" thing that has a dozen civs on the other side of the ocean that no one over here has even met, who suddenly decide they're qualified to tell me that I can't have access to the ocean.

Wait, I can't keep building spies?  I have a "trade route" limit?  but, why?  I don't understand what this is supposed to mean?  I guess I'm not allowed to build it, so they won't come.  Guess I'll build another stupid district that'll take 120 moves and do me no good until I build 2 or 3 buildings therein, which'll take another 100 moves, or maybe only 50 now because the cities grown.

So there's no Leonardo's Workshop, and no glorious moment when all your warriors become musketmen.  I get that, OK.  That was kind of dumb -- but I *loved* it.  This city garrison system is just a kludge to take away what was kind of a key part of Civ 2; that you had to defend your own cities.  Now, the AI just weirdly randomly decides how capable your city defenses are?  Huh?

"Theological combat"?  Is that some kind of a joke?

The end eventually comes.  I lost, in a "diplomatic victory" to that same dumb civilization whose capital had been independent for 1000 years, by one point, thanks to this "World Congress" thing where everybody submits proposals, but no one gets to look at the other proposals before voting.  Which totally makes sense.  I'm the only civ that has flight, and in hindsight I probably should've started sweeping the globe when I got that, but oh well.  The "winner" hasn't even discovered electricity, FFS, whereas I'm gearing up for a space race with me, myself, and I as competitors.

OK, I did like the rock band thing.  Credit where due.

I'm just trying to remember that, in the long run, I'm just pushing around electronic impulses in a few microchips.  It just doesn't matter.  Why did I play this?  Why?

*old man yells at cloud*

I chose an archipelago map, because I loved sending those poor triremes out to be the first to make contact with other continents and cultures. What does the game do? Plops me down in the middle of a huge continent, which literally stretches from pole to pole, and not only that, but puts me on a lake, which it calls a "bay" but is not, with other independent cities and civs hogging all the land near the actual ocean, and of course none of those idiots build cities on the two isthmuses that would allow ships to get in and out of the lake. There was another nearby civilization that just could not get it's crap together. Another player came and conquered its capital, but could not keep it due to "loyalty", and it rebelled. And just sat there as an independent city for 1000 years. An independent city, mind you, that I could not actually send envoys to or do anything useful with. Just sitting there. Since it's on the other side of the continent with access to the ocean on the other side of the world from what I have, I decide it's time to take them out. My control lasts, like, 3 moves, when loyalty takes it back to its original owner. This had to be a bug. I really do not understand how the whole suzerain system works. So, these other cities just sit there, doing nothing, until I pay them to take their armies? Huh? That's the only thing they're good for, other than blocking my access to the ocean? I dutifully waited until other civs made the most convenient ones suzerains, then wars began, and I took them over -- only to immediately get dinged by some "World Congress" thing that has a dozen civs on the other side of the ocean that no one over here has even met, who suddenly decide they're qualified to tell me that I can't have access to the ocean. Wait, I can't keep building spies? I have a "trade route" limit? but, why? I don't understand what this is supposed to mean? I guess I'm not allowed to build it, so they won't come. Guess I'll build another stupid district that'll take 120 moves and do me no good until I build 2 or 3 buildings therein, which'll take another 100 moves, or maybe only 50 now because the cities grown. So there's no Leonardo's Workshop, and no glorious moment when all your warriors become musketmen. I get that, OK. That was kind of dumb -- but I loved it. This city garrison system is just a kludge to take away what was kind of a key part of Civ 2 that you had to defend your own cities. Now, the AI just weirdly randomly decides how capable your city defenses are? Huh? "Theological combat"? Is that some kind of a joke? The end eventually comes. I lost, in a "diplomatic victory" to that same dumb civilization whose capital had been independent for 1000 years, by one point, thanks to this "World Congress" thing where everybody submits proposals, but no one gets to look at the other proposals before voting. Which totally makes sense. I'm the only civ that has flight, and in hindsight I probably should've started sweeping the globe when I got that, but oh well. The "winner" hasn't even discovered electricity, FFS, whereas I'm gearing up for a space race with me, myself, and I as competitors. OK, I did like the rock band thing. Credit where due. I'm just trying to remember that, in the long run, I'm just pushing around electronic impulses in a few microchips. It just doesn't matter. Why did I play this? Why? old man yells at cloud

review:
Complex without much in the way of reward. Yes, you can keep going for hours but when you stop it doesn't feel like you've achieved anything. Every mechanic you could possibly think of is in the game, but they don't really make a coherent whole. I have never felt loth to stop playing, nor have I ever been excited to get back to the game.

For the first year after I bought the game I was unable to play because of a call stack error where the spaghetti code would dive into a stack of subroutines and then overwrite the first one and get completely lost when I tried to join any multiplayer game. My PC was perfectly able to run it in singleplayer and all the specs were up to standard, but only on a different PC did the problem go away. Test the game quickly if you do end up buying it to see if you need to refund the hefty price tag or if it will work for you.

review:
(3.8/5)

Civilization 6 is a wonderful grand strategy game that makes you feel like you're in control of an empire, planning trade routes, exchanging various goods with the other players, and constructing wonders for the world to see.

While it's not the most overwhelming Grand Strategy game I've played or is available, it can be a bit daunting at first as you must weed through district adjacencies, unit combat support, and other mechanics. Finding an easy beginner civ can also be challenging, especially with all of the DLC.

While the game is perfectly serviceable without any of the DLC, Rise & Fall and Gathering Storm change the game in very impactful ways, meaning that if you spend a lot of time without these and decide to pick them up at a later date (preferably on sale), a lot of your experience will not translate over.

As this game has a lot of DLC, some of which is pretty much mandatory for the full experience, I sadly cannot give it a 4*+, however it is well worth your time when on sale.

review:
As a single player game there is enough going on that each new combination and random map makes it interesting. I hate the French all they do is spy and steal money, but otherwise the character of each leader plays a real role in development and style of play. I recommend but caution that you should always adjust the win conditions. More than once I have had higher difficulty wins by scores that leap ahead in the last few turns.
A lot of fun, some frustration, and the difficulty levels genuinely force alterations to play that make it a real challenge. Get the character packs because it makes it even more fun. No turn limit huge maps are awesome!

review:
My review of this game is not against its gameplay. it is generally fine. The UI is terrible without mods and some of the gameplay choices are a mix of good and bad compared to previous entries. 
My main issue with this game is in its thematic direction. The characters all look like clowns and the game generally just looks ugly. The cartoony style is not appealing.
While the Civ series has never been historically accurate it would typically try to take the material at hand seriously. Comparing things like the art direction and tech/civics quotes compared to previous entries is probably the best example. 
Many of the quotes are much more cynical and grumpy as you approach the modern era. Many quotes are along the lines of "Snarky and possibly witty take about loose historical ideas" that redditors would frame and put on the exposed brick wall of their studio apartment.
The game has an entire DLC dedicated to climate change; while the way people treat our environment is a real issue the presentation of it in this game is odd because it wants to make this an important gameplay factor going into the industrial era. All it really does is force you to watch cutscenes of things happening that you cannot control. I am sure some will defend it but there are better ways to handle these themes than the way Civ 6 handles them.

review:
It's not terrible but I don't see this as an upgrade over CIV 5 or even 4. You're freed from the happiness constrains of CIV 5 just to be constrained by a million different things. The whole district and governor system is just a lot of extra steps and micromanagement to achieve the same things you were already doing in the previous games.  At first what seems like options are actual chains that tie you down in a certain direction for the next 100 turns. I find turn to turn I'm making even fewer decisions than I am in previous games.

Also the art style is just disgusting imo.  I shouldn't prefer the graphics of a 20 year old game over this.

review:
这游戏是真的上头。我前两天跟同学一起开黑，本来就想玩一会儿，结果直接干到凌晨四点多，还感觉才过了半小时。早上醒来发现十二点了，早八和早十都不用去了，体育课直接挂科。脑子里就剩一个念头：还得是《文明6》。

说实话，玩这游戏就像在真实地经营一个文明。一开始就一个小城几个兵，慢慢探索地图、选位置建城、搞科技、搞文化、搞军队，看着自己的国家一步步壮大，那个成就感确实很实在。而且每一回合你都得做选择，是优先科技还是军队，是扩张还是稳住经济，是跟别人合作还是直接翻脸。尤其城市规划那块特别有意思，区域得放在合适的位置，地形、资源、相邻加成都要提前想好，不是随便堆建筑就行的。

但这游戏也特别能让你“心态崩”。我开最低难度，甘地都能开局打我，还把我罗马给端了。从公元前4000年到公元300年一直在跟野蛮人死磕，还被围城围到破防。更别说那些AI了，你不打它骂你废物，你打它就说你好战，你派使者它就威胁你，它打你是正义，你打回去就是不讲武德。有次我被勒索得受不了，国库里明明二十多颗核弹，丢了一颗还被说搞偷袭。我就纳闷了，你69岁的骑士非要撞我现代坦克，还问我好不好？那你别来惹我啊。

反正一句话：如果你觉得时间太多没地方花，买它，玩几局你就知道什么叫光阴似箭了。也有人调侃说“10点开一局，9点59打完”，懂的都懂。至于抖M嘛，喜欢被AI从开局骂到结尾的，那更是必入。

总的来说，这游戏不靠快节奏刺激人，但那种“再玩一回合”的魔力太强了。换一个文明、换一个胜利目标、再随机生成一张新地图，又是全新一局。喜欢动脑子，喜欢经营和长线规划的朋友，真可以试试。

review_english: This game is really awesome. I played black with my classmates two days ago. I originally wanted to play for a while, but I worked until about 4 o'clock in the morning. It felt like only half an hour had passed. I woke up in the morning and found that it was 12 o'clock. The sense of achievement is indeed very real, and every round you have to make a choice, whether to prioritize technology or the army, whether to expand or stabilize the economy, whether to cooperate with others or directly fall out with each other. The urban planning part is particularly interesting. The area must be placed in the right location, terrain, resources, and adjacent bonuses must be thought out in advance. It is not just a random pile of buildings. But this game can also make you lose your mentality. I played the lowest difficulty, and Gandhi could beat me at the beginning and even killed me in Rome. From 4000 B.C. to 300 A.D., we were at war with the barbarians, and we were besieged until the defenses were broken. Not to mention those AIs. If you don’t fight them, they call you trash. If you fight them, they will call you warlike. If you send a messenger, they will threaten you. It’s justice to fight you. If you fight back, you just don’t have martial ethics. Once I was extorted so much that I couldn’t stand it. There were more than 20 nuclear bombs in the treasury. I lost one and they were accused of sneak attacks. I was puzzled. Your 69-year-old knight insisted on ramming my modern tank. You asked me if I'm okay, so don't mess with me. Anyway, if you think you have too much time and no place to spend it, buy it. Play a few games and you will know what time flies. Some people also joked that a game starts at 10 o'clock and ends at 9:59. Everyone knows it. As for shaking M, if you like to be scolded by the AI from the beginning to the end, it is a must. In general, this game does not rely on fast pace to stimulate people, but the magic of playing another round is too strong. Change to another civilization. Change the victory goal and randomly generate a new map. It's a whole new game. Friends who like to use their brains, management and long-term planning can really try it.

review:
Since I was a kid, I’ve always wanted to get into RPGs, simulations, turn-based, and turn-based strategy/tactics games, as I’ve always been drawn to them, but I just found them very complicated. A lot of things just went over my head. I would play games like Sims, Risk, and Bloons TD to scratch the itch, and while very fun, I still wanted more.

In 2020, I would heavily get into RPG turn-based combat games like FFN, Risk, XCOM, Slay the Spire, Persona, and Baldur's Gate. I hated how long it took me to give them a chance, and I don't want to repeat the same thing with turn-based strategy and RTS games. The games give off the same vibes as the RPGs, but just feel overwhelming as hell. I’ve bought several, but every time I boot them up, I feel like I’m reading a history textbook mixed with a spreadsheet simulator. There are just so many systems, mechanics, menus, and things to keep track of. They feel like shows that are on season 20, like, who the hell is Stussy? The barrier to entry is just too high. I feel like I need to familiarize myself with the older games. Despite all that, at the same time, I’m still weirdly drawn to them. RTS aside, there's just something special about building up a civilization, managing cities, going to war, making alliances, and watching your empire evolve. I just know that these are one of those games where, once the mechanics finally click for me, I’m probably going to get addicted and dump thousands of hours into them.

I could be wrong, but Civ seems like the type of game you can chill and play solo while listening to music or talking with friends. I’m also curious to know if it’s fun with friends too, kind of like games like Monopoly Plus and Risk, where everyone can just hop in, mess around, betray each other, and have a good time together. I’m just overwhelmed at the moment, and I don't really know what to do. Like, should I go back and play the earlier games, or is there a video that explains everything...?

review:
Systems and Depth: 9.5/10 (Extremely rich in regional planning, government systems, and governor mechanics; easy to start but hard to master)  
War and Conquest: 8.5/10 (Tactical gameplay in the early and mid-game is excellent, but late-game high-level strikes are overly cumbersome)  
AI and Diplomacy: 6.0/10 (AI military and diplomatic logic is still a weak point; high difficulty relies entirely on cheat bonuses)  
Late-Game Experience: 7.0/10 (The 'trash time' is too long, workload is heavy, lacks endgame challenges)  
Addictiveness: 10/10 ('Just one more turn' magic is unbeatable, a true time killer)  

In 400 hours, I have led Roman legions to conquer the Eurasian continent, and I have also used Age of Exploration fleets to break open the doors to the Americas; I have calculated every patch of frozen land in the cold wind, and hesitated over pressing the nuclear button on whether to sink Venice.  
Civilization VI is not a perfect game; its AI sometimes acts like an idiot, and its late game sometimes feels like a factory line for clocking in. Yet it remains the grandest, most detailed 4X strategy game currently on the market, best fulfilling players' fantasy of 'creating history.' It condenses thousands of years of human civilization into a hexagonal board, allowing you to experience the epic feel from making fire to exploring space.  

If you have not tried the Civilization series yet, or just want to buy a game that can provide hundreds of hours of play, buying it is absolutely worth it. But please be sure: before clicking 'Start Game,' set your alarm, prepare liver supplements, and forgive yourself for not getting out of bed tomorrow morning.  

I think I can play just one more game. Just one, I’ll go to sleep after finishing this turn…

review:
One of my favorite games to pick up and put down endlessly. The game mechanics are so natural that I could never forget them and never really needed to learn most of them. The experience of going through a full round is incredibly fulfilling, I can never get enough. I have some weeks played through well over a dozen games of this.

My only complaint (which is now rendered less relevant given the frequency of updates) is the large dependence on paid dlc. I first played on my Nintendo Switch for some 80 hours, but my enjoyment only reached peak levels when buying the heavily discounted Anthology version here on Steam. Without the DLC, the game feels incomplete, which may serve as a compliment to the DLC or an insult to the base game. The jury's still out.

review:
Devs cooked and forgot the recipe between Civ VI and Civ VII.

I found Civ VI a little overwhelming at first as someone new to turn-based large scale strategy games, (though found it an excellent warmup for Stellaris), but after a few short playthroughs, I got it down.

I know a lot of people don't like the clay-like graphics, but personally, I think it makes everything softer and easier on the eyes when playing for 5+ hour sessions. The game has lovely pacing and even some storytelling. UI isn't too cluttered and you have the option to micromanage cities if you want/need.

My only real criticism is the odd currency inflation which happens with units when you hoard currency a little too much. You almost have to constantly be expending currency to keep units at a stable price for on-demand purchase.

Lots of DLC's, lots of differing perks per Civ, lots of game customization and control, and lots of fun. I highly recommend this game if you enjoy strategy games, and I recommend this above all other Civ games. Also, like I said earlier, this is a pretty good prelude to Stellaris in practicing grand strategy and attention.

Also, multiplayer sucks. I tried it and it's unfortunately hella buggy. Plus the game takes twice as long between two, as less stuff is automated between you and other players. Multiplayer is defo a gripe of mine.

review:
[b]C'est probablement le jeu auquel j'ai le plus joué, toutes plateformes confondues.[/b]
En solo ou en multijoueur, le jeu offre des parties toutes différentes les unes des autres, notamment grâce à la diversité de dirigeants et de modes de jeux.
Je ne regrette absolue pas d'avoir investi au fil du temps pour obtenir tous les DLCs et Extentions. 
Le jeu ne demande pas beaucoup de ressources à l'ordinateur, même avec les graphismes au niveau moyen.
Et grâce aux informations données sur les différents dirigeants, ça apporte aussi un côté éducatif au jeu, qui m'a appris pas mal de choses.

Un des meilleurs jeux de stratégie au tour par tour de mon point de vue.

review:
Civilization VI — 8/10

Civilization VI to jedna z tych gier, które potrafią wciągnąć na dziesiątki godzin bez większego problemu. Każda rozgrywka wygląda inaczej, bo wszystko zależy od cywilizacji, mapy i tego, jak poprowadzisz rozwój swojego imperium.

Gameplay jest bardzo rozbudowany — budowanie miast, rozwój technologii, dyplomacja i wojny tworzą naprawdę głęboką strategię. To gra typu „jeszcze jedna tura i kończę”, która kończy się o 3 w nocy.

Optymalizacja jest dobra, gra działa stabilnie, chociaż przy bardzo dużych mapach i późnej fazie gry potrafi trochę zwolnić.

Zalety:

* ogromna grywalność i replayability
* bardzo rozbudowana strategia
* dużo cywilizacji i stylów gry
* wciągający „jeszcze jedna tura” efekt
* dobra oprawa wizualna i czytelny interfejs

Wady:

* potrafi być bardzo czasochłonna
* DLC mocno rozbudowują grę (i kosztują)
* późna faza gry bywa wolniejsza
* wysoki próg wejścia dla nowych graczy

Ogólnie świetna strategia, jedna z najlepszych w swoim gatunku, ale trzeba mieć dużo czasu, żeby ją naprawdę docenić.
review_clean: Civilization VI 8 10 Civilization VI to jedna z tych gier, które potrafią wciągnąć na dziesiątki godzin bez większego problemu. Każda rozgrywka wygląda inaczej, bo wszystko zależy od cywilizacji, mapy i tego, jak poprowadzisz rozwój swojego imperium. Gameplay jest bardzo rozbudowany budowanie miast, rozwój technologii, dyplomacja i wojny tworzą naprawdę głęboką strategię. To gra typu jeszcze jedna tura i kończę , która kończy się o 3 w nocy. Optymalizacja jest dobra, gra działa stabilnie, chociaż przy bardzo dużych mapach i późnej fazie gry potrafi trochę zwolnić. Zalety ogromna grywalność i replayability bardzo rozbudowana strategia dużo cywilizacji i stylów gry wciągający jeszcze jedna tura efekt dobra oprawa wizualna i czytelny interfejs Wady potrafi być bardzo czasochłonna DLC mocno rozbudowują grę (i kosztują) późna faza gry bywa wolniejsza wysoki próg wejścia dla nowych graczy Ogólnie świetna strategia, jedna z najlepszych w swoim gatunku, ale trzeba mieć dużo czasu, żeby ją naprawdę docenić.
review_english: Civilization VI 8 10 Civilization VI is one of those games that can absorb you for dozens of hours without any major problems. Each game is different, because everything depends on the civilization, the map and how you develop your empire. The gameplay is very extensive - city building, technology development, diplomacy and war create a really deep strategy. It's a one more turn and I'm done game that ends at 3 a.m. Optimization is good, the game runs stably, although it can slow down a bit with very large maps and late game. Advantages: huge playability and replayability, very extensive strategy, many civilizations and game styles, addictive one-turn effect, good visuals and clear interface. Disadvantages: it can be very time-consuming. DLCs expand the game a lot (and are expensive), the late game phase can be slower, a high entry barrier for new players. Overall, a great strategy, one of the best in its genre, but you need a lot of time to really appreciate it.

review: 妈妈，人生要真是《文明6》就好了… 每一步选择都铺成文明的轨迹，种下的农田会在岁月里长出粮食，凿开的矿山会在时光里流出财富。哪怕开局只有一个移民和侦察兵，也能在千年后建起横跨大陆的帝国；哪怕遭遇蛮族的劫掠、火山的喷发、瘟疫的蔓延，只要城市还在，就有卷土重来的可能。 付出的每一滴汗水都有回响：研究科技能点亮时代的星火，积累文化能孕育思想的光芒，发展信仰能凝聚文明的脊梁。失败从不是终点，只是下一局的序章——你可以重新选择领袖，重新规划疆域，在另一条时间线里，让文明走向更辉煌的远方。 就算你只想在世界角落种满庄稼、养满牲畜，不去争夺胜利，不去扩张版图，历史也会温柔地记下：这是一个热爱和平、耕耘大地的文明，它在自己的时区里，缓慢而坚定地生长。 《文明6》就像一个可以反复重启的文明沙盘，我躲进去，好像就能暂时放下现实里的迷茫与挫败，在像素与回合里，重新学会相信“努力终有回响”。但我也知道，我不能永远停在回合制的温柔里，现实的文明，还要由我亲手书写下一个千年。

review_english: Mom, if life were really Civilization 6, every choice would be paved with the trajectory of civilization. The farmland planted will grow food in the years, and the mines dug will flow out wealth in time. Even if there is only one immigrant and scout at the beginning, a continent-spanning empire can be built in a thousand years. Even if it encounters barbarian plunder, volcanic eruptions, and the spread of plague, as long as the city still exists, there is a possibility of a comeback. Every drop of sweat paid has an echo. Researching technology can light up the spark of the times. Accumulating culture can breed the light of thought. Developing faith can condense the backbone of civilization. Failure is never the end, it is just the prologue of the next game. You can re-select leaders, re-plan the territory, and let the civilization move to a more glorious distance in another timeline. Even if you just want to plant crops and livestock in the corner of the world and do not compete for victory or expand the territory, history will tenderly record it. This is a civilization that loves peace and cultivates the earth. It grows slowly and firmly in its own time zone. Civilization 6 It's like a civilized sandbox that can be restarted repeatedly. When I hide in it, I seem to be able to temporarily let go of the confusion and frustration in reality. In pixels and turns, I learn to believe again. My efforts will eventually pay off. But I also know that I can't stay in the gentleness of the turn system forever. The real civilization needs to be written by my own hands for the next millennium.

review:
Civilization VI is the kind of game I should probably recommend people stay away from. Seriously. This is not me being dramatic or trying to exaggerate for effect: this game will consume your time with terrifying efficiency. One moment you're founding your first city, the next you realize eight hours disappeared and you've achieved absolutely nothing productive in real life. Whether that sounds horrifying or wonderful depends entirely on the kind of person you are.

As a 4X strategy game, though, it’s exceptional. Civilization VI is incredibly easy to pick up, which is honestly part of the problem. Its mechanics are intuitive enough that you can start engaging with the deeper systems surprisingly fast, and before long you’re trapped in the classic “one more turn” cycle the series is infamous for. The pacing is quick, the systems are polished, and overall it’s one of the most accessible strategy games in the genre while still offering a lot of depth for players willing to master it.

The frustration comes from the snowball effect, which is absolutely brutal. You can spend hours building what feels like a solid empire, only to realize the game was effectively lost back around turn 60 and you just didn’t know it yet. Recovering from a bad start is difficult, especially against aggressive civilizations or the AI, which sometimes feels less intelligent and more chemically enhanced. If left unchecked, it’ll happily sprint toward late-game military dominance while you’re still trying to understand why your economy collapsed three eras ago.

Still, Civilization VI is addictive for a reason. It’s a masterclass in turn-based 4X design: easy to learn, difficult to master, and dangerously good at making time disappear. The downside is that losing after several hours can feel genuinely exhausting, especially once you realize how unforgiving the game can be if you fall behind early.

In the end, the game is addictive as hell and a masterclass of turn-based 4X strategy games. I don't think there are many games of this type as easy to learn as this one, but the cost comes as frustration and time wasted, which will undeniably kick in after hours of playing just for you to lose. If you're willing to commit to that, this is an otherwise almost perfect game for that. If you have less time, stay away from it, seriously, because it'll become an unhealthy habit that will easily waste your time for long periods. And I think I'm in this latter category right now.

review:
Как по мне 6 часть в результате получилась довольно спорной.

За что можно похвалить:
- механика районов
- система политических карточек
- механика ГГшек
- система великих людей
- особенности цивилизаций

Негативные моменты:
- чрезвычайно тупой ИИ
- чрезвычайно плохой неткод (выкидывает даже с лобби, не говоря уже об самой игре)
- мультяшная графика (выглядит как насмехательство над историей)
- после всех дополнений и апдейтов - слишком перегружена различными механиками и постройками (особенно сильно ощущается на поздних этапах игры). Абсолютно  инородными выглядят механики и постройки связанные с природой и экологией (например затопления, которые просто забирают клетки; бури, которые являются абсолютно случайными и ломают как клетки так и все здания в городе и игрок никак не может на это повлиять и другие события). Также лишним выглядит механика губернаторов.
- плохо реализовано всё что связано с дипломатией (механики и постройки)

Суммируя, можно сказать, что в первые годы после выпуска данная часть была впринципе вполне играбельной частью серии Цивилизации с рядом неплохих плюсов, которые стоили того что бы терпеть все минусы. Но по состоянию на 2026, игра перенасыщена большим и явно лишним количеством элементов. В предыдущую, 5 часть, явно приятнее играть.

Также лишним выглядит механика губернаторов. - плохо реализовано всё что связано с дипломатией (механики и постройки) Суммируя, можно сказать, что в первые годы после выпуска данная часть была впринципе вполне играбельной частью серии Цивилизации с рядом неплохих плюсов, которые стоили того что бы терпеть все минусы. Но по состоянию на 2026, игра перенасыщена большим и явно лишним количеством элементов. В предыдущую, 5 часть, явно приятнее играть.
review_english: As for me, part 6 turned out to be quite controversial. What can be praised for - district mechanics - political card system - GGshek mechanics - great people system - features of civilizations Negative points - extremely stupid AI - extremely bad netcode (kicks you out even from the lobby, not to mention the game itself) - cartoonish graphics (looks like a mockery of history) - after all the additions and updates - too overloaded with various mechanics and buildings (especially noticeable in the later stages of the game). Mechanics and buildings related to nature and ecology look completely alien (for example, flooding, which simply takes away storm cells, which are completely random and break both cells and all buildings in the city and the player cannot influence this in any way, and other events). The governor mechanics also look superfluous. - everything related to diplomacy (mechanics and buildings) is poorly implemented. Summarizing, we can say that in the first years after release, this part was, in principle, a completely playable part of the Civilization series with a number of good advantages that were worth enduring all the disadvantages. But as of 2026, the game is oversaturated with a large and clearly unnecessary number of elements. The previous, part 5, is clearly more enjoyable to play.

The "fog of war" is the exact same color as the discovered areas that are out of your render distance, so it's hard to tell what you've explored at a glance. They throw a bunch of new stuff at you from the beginning, and it's impossible to know what's going on because the text is TINY. I could barely read it. There is a setting to fix it, theoretically, but it was stuck at 100% with no way to change it. When the social policies thing popped up, I knew I was going to give up. Maybe if I had started on this one, but I think I'll stick with 5.

Also, I'm not normally a person who cares about graphics, but oh my god they were so bad! It's this weird mix of hyper realistic and cartoony with a 2D old timey map. It all just clashed in an incredibly displeasing way.

review: Наверно это худшая игра из всей серии. Совершенно не интуитивная, не прозрачная, с кучей скрытых механик о которых нет никакой информации. К примеру ты можешь отобрать у варваров несколько рабочих, а на следующий ход все нации тебе сделают осуждение и объявят войну, При том, что ты находишься у себя на острове, карта открыта не вся, у тебя 5 городов и ты ни с кем не воюешь, а по технологической ветке где-то в самом конце. И при этом нет никакой информации за что тебя осудили, и почему тебе объявили войну. С перемещением юнитов и картой тоже очень весело, стоит город, через одну клетку у тебя лучник, но ты не можешь атаковать, хотя между вами нет каких ни рек ни гор, ни даже построек, просто поле, но для атаки тебе надо стать на другую клетку, и потратить при этом 2 хода, а город тебя атакует и за 1 атаку снимает 50% hp юнита, но даже если ты успеешь к нему подойти то своей атакой снимаешь городу по 1-2% hp. Насколько я люблю цивку еще с 3 версии, несмотря на их бред с лучниками которые сбивают самолет и копейщиками которые уничтожают полностью здоровый танк, но эта часть это прям худшая из худших.

review_english: This is probably the worst game in the entire series. Completely unintuitive, non-transparent, with a bunch of hidden mechanics about which there is no information. For example, you can take away several workers from the barbarians, and on the next turn all the nations will condemn you and declare war. Even though you are on your island, not the whole map is open, you have 5 cities and you are not at war with anyone, but on the technology branch somewhere at the very end. And at the same time, there is no information about why you were convicted, and why war was declared on you. Moving units and the map is also a lot of fun, there is a city, you have an archer in one cell, but you can’t attack, although there are no rivers, mountains, or even buildings between you, just a field, but to attack you need to stand on another cell and spend 2 moves, and the city attacks you and for 1 attack removes 50 hp from the unit, but even if you manage to approach it, then with your attack you remove the city by 1-2 hp. As much as I love the civ since version 3, despite their nonsense with archers who shoot down a plane and spearmen who destroy a completely healthy tank, but this part is just the worst of the worst.

review:
[u][b]-Quick Review-[/b][/u]
From the dawn of humanity, Civilizations have risen and fallen. Egypt, Greece, Rome, Teddy Roosevelt. Onetime the Norwegians conquered Japan by spreading the power of God. Do you all remember that one time the Aztec’s launched a rocket into space?
Oh yeah, I dropped my profound inspirational rhetoric to embrace video games absurdity really quickly, didn’t I? Civilization VI was developed by Firaxis Games and released in 2016, Civilization VI is a turn-based strategy game where you suddenly lose track of time as you continue to click “Next turn” only to discover it is now 3:00 in the morning and you need to get up in a few hours to go to work.

[u][b]Review Video:[/b][/u]
If you don't like reading, or are curious about watching gameplay check out my video here:
https://youtu.be/oaVZ8ntSJJ4

[u][b]-Detailed breakdown review-[/b][/u]
[b]Gameplay:[/b] A typical Civilization session will start with a settler and a combat unit. After you build your city and you send your warrior off to explore, then you’ll explore and build a few scouts, a building or two, then oh look you can build that wonder, oh dang I found a city state and another civilization. Then as you keep playing the other civilization will get pissed off at you because you haven’t been building a navy because we’re all landlocked on this map, then they denounce you, and sometimes they declare war on you, and when you’ve pummeled all their armies into dust and taken over all their cities, the other civilizations accuse you of being a warmonger.
WELL GUESS WHAT, I GUESS THIS IS A SELF FULFILLING PROPHECY! I’LL BE THE WARMONGER MONSTER, YOU ALL KEEP ACCUSING ME OF BEING. I DIDN’T START THIS WAR BUT I SURE AS HELL WILL END IT.
Sorry, I’m working through things.
That’s how you end up getting hooked on a game of civilization which was only supposed to go for a few hours but now way past your bedtime.

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3591873421

I think the best place to start with Civilization games is talking about the win conditions. How do you win in Civilization VI?  By Domination, Science, Culture, Religion, and Score.
In Civ 6 you don’t need to take all of your enemies' cities to win, just their capital cities.
Science has several steps to it, first you need to unlock the technology toward the end of the track, then you need to build a space city district and finish 3 different projects, launch a satellite, land of the moon, establish a mars colony, and then lastly launch an Exoplanet Expedition to win. (So, you’ll need high science and then the economy/production to be able to finish those projects before anyone else)
I think the Cultural victory is kinda weird, it’s all about having tourists visit your civilization. It’s also the main reason I turn to violence in my play sessions because much like religion victories it’s sneaky if you’re not paying attention to it.
Speaking of Religious Victory, that’s a thing now. You can win by converting other nations to your religion. But religious Victory hits differently when you make a custom religion called “You F Snakes”, and every time you spread your religion you see that text pop up on screen.
Lastly, Score Victory is the game saying “Hey, why didn’t you win already? Now you’re out of turns and we’re going to tally up who was doing the best.”

[b:]What’s New from Civ 5 to Civ 6?[/b] Other than the already mentioned “Religious Victory” condition of converting all other nations to your religion, and also different types of religious units which can now engage in religious combat by summoning lightning from the heavens to smite their enemies.
The next biggest change is the “Unstacking of cities”. In previous civilization games your city was held to the hex-tile it was settled on. In Civ 6, your city spreads out more, you build special “districts”, so if you want to get more science you need to build a Campus first then build upgrades for your campus. Or an Encampment district where you can build a Barracks to provide new units with more combat experience.
One of the more challenging parts of this change is knowing where to place these districts because they get bonuses for being placed near certain titles. For example a campus district gets bonus science for being placed next to mountain tiles. But not only do you have different districts, you also place wonders on tiles. To restate things: in Civ games prior to 6, you build a city and then improve the tiles around your city. In Civ 6, you build your city, and the space around your city will also be used by that city for districts and wonders.
Next biggest change I’d say would be the addition of a Social Policies tree. Just like with science in other Civ games, where you need to research technology in a certain order, you can’t put research towards electricity when you just started a new game and haven’t learned pottery. 
So too you now need to work on social policies to unlock better governments with better perks. Also, governments don’t just give static benefits. I remember selecting my government in the past based on your play style and unlocking better governments with science… now to unlock the next government you need to select social policies along a tree.
The whole process of selecting Technology and Social policies is more dynamic too, because most of them have conditions which can be met to get a boost towards that research to unlock it even faster!

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3610883715
 
[b]Conclusion:[/b] I forget how technically complex Civilization games are. It’s kind of hard to narrow down and explain key gameplay mechanics and changes between the different versions. Or maybe it’s just that I haven’t pumped in enough thousands of hours to understand the system well enough to articulate those details?
…Maybe we can just split the difference and go with “Civ 6 is pretty easy to pick up and learn (adequate tutorials throughout the game), however Civ 6 takes a long time to master because of all its different systems and mechanics.”
For what it’s worth, I’ve enjoyed playing the game. I spent more time in Civ 5 and still prefer it’s gameplay, and maybe if I ever get around to buying and playing the DLC for Civ 6 I might change my opinion in the future.
Thanks for taking the time to read my review!

I think the best place to start with Civilization games is talking about the win conditions. How do you win in Civilization VI? By Domination, Science, Culture, Religion, and Score. In Civ 6 you don t need to take all of your enemies' cities to win, just their capital cities. Science has several steps to it, first you need to unlock the technology toward the end of the track, then you need to build a space city district and finish 3 different projects, launch a satellite, land of the moon, establish a mars colony, and then lastly launch an Exoplanet Expedition to win. (So, you ll need high science and then the economy production to be able to finish those projects before anyone else) I think the Cultural victory is kinda weird, it s all about having tourists visit your civilization. It s also the main reason I turn to violence in my play sessions because much like religion victories it s sneaky if you re not paying attention to it.
